title: Machine Learning Lecture 32: Boosting (Cornell CS4780)
summary: This Cornell CS4780 lecture covers the Boosting ensemble method, focusing on how weak learners are sequentially combined to form a strong classifier. Key topics include AdaBoost, the theoretical foundations of boosting via exponential loss minimization, and how sample weights are updated iteratively to focus on misclassified examples. The lecture provides rigorous mathematical derivations making it useful for understanding why boosting works and its connections to forward stagewise additive modeling.

title: Mixed Data Clustering: Survey and Challenges
summary: This paper surveys clustering methods designed for mixed data types (numerical and categorical variables), addressing limitations of traditional homogeneous-data clustering techniques in the big data era. It introduces a novel clustering approach grounded in pretopological spaces, emphasizing hierarchical and explainable algorithms that produce interpretable results. The paper benchmarks the proposed method against classical numerical clustering algorithms and existing pretopological approaches, providing insights into performance on heterogeneous datasets.

title: Learning from Imbalanced Data Sets
summary: A comprehensive textbook covering the theory and practice of imbalanced classification, including cost-sensitive learning, data-level preprocessing (e.g., oversampling/undersampling), algorithm-level solutions, and ensemble methods. It addresses both binary and multi-class imbalanced problems, data intrinsic characteristics (overlap, small disjuncts), and extends to data streams, Big Data scalability, and non-classical problems like semi-supervised learning, multilabel classification, and ordinal regression. Practical software libraries and case studies (fraud detection, disease diagnosis) are included, making it a strong reference for applying imbalanced learning in real-world data science workflows.

title: M5 Forecasting Accuracy Competition – 1st Place Solution
summary: This writeup details the winning solution for the M5 Forecasting Accuracy Kaggle competition, which focused on hierarchical retail sales forecasting across Walmart stores. The solution likely employs gradient boosting models (e.g., LightGBM) with extensive feature engineering including lag features, rolling statistics, and calendar/price effects. It is highly valuable as a reference for real-world time series forecasting at scale, demonstrating techniques for handling hierarchical structure, uncertainty quantification, and ensemble strategies.

title: How to Determine Which Distribution Fits Your Data Best
summary: This resource provides a practical guide to distribution fitting in R using the fitdistrplus package, covering how to identify candidate distributions via the descdist function (skewness-kurtosis plot), fit distributions like Weibull, Normal, and Gamma, and compare them using AIC and QQ-plots. It also addresses the critical limitation of standard KS-tests with estimated parameters, demonstrating a simulation-based approach to generate a valid null distribution for the KS-statistic. The guide emphasizes that the goal is finding parsimonious approximate descriptions rather than proving an exact distributional match.

title: Explainable ML (1/8): Introduction to Interpretable Machine Learning
summary: This is the first video in an 8-part lecture series on Explainable and Interpretable Machine Learning, likely from Hung-yi Lee's ML course at NTU. The series covers techniques for understanding and explaining black-box model predictions, which is critical for trust, debugging, and regulatory compliance. Topics across the series typically include local and global explanation methods, model-agnostic approaches, and intrinsically interpretable models.

title: Best Techniques and Metrics for Imbalanced Datasets
summary: This Kaggle notebook provides a comprehensive guide to handling class imbalance in machine learning classification tasks. It covers resampling strategies (oversampling with SMOTE, undersampling), cost-sensitive learning, and ensemble methods tailored for imbalanced data. Evaluation metrics appropriate for imbalanced settings are emphasized, including precision-recall curves, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient, making it a practical reference for avoiding misleading accuracy-based assessments.

title: Modelling Recurrent Events: A Tutorial for Analysis in Epidemiology
summary: This tutorial covers statistical methods for analysing recurrent time-to-event data, where a participant can experience the same event multiple times. It explores conditional models (Andersen-Gill, PWP total time, PWP gap time), marginal means/rates models, frailty models, and multi-state models, explaining when each is appropriate and how to interpret results. The paper demonstrates implementation in SAS, Stata, and R using bladder cancer recurrence and respiratory infection datasets. It is particularly useful for understanding within-subject correlation adjustments and selecting the right modelling strategy for repeated event survival data.

title: Credit Fraud Detection: Dealing with Imbalanced Datasets
summary: This Kaggle notebook tackles credit card fraud detection using the classic imbalanced dataset where fraudulent transactions represent a tiny minority of cases. It covers techniques for handling class imbalance including undersampling, oversampling with SMOTE, and proper cross-validation strategies to avoid data leakage. The notebook demonstrates logistic regression and tree-based classifiers, emphasizing evaluation metrics appropriate for imbalanced data such as precision-recall curves, F1-score, and AUPRC rather than accuracy.

title: American Express - Default Prediction (Kaggle Competition)
summary: A Kaggle competition challenging participants to predict whether a customer will default on their credit card balance using a large-scale real-world dataset from American Express. The dataset contains aggregated customer profile features spanning delinquency, spend, payment, balance, and risk variables over time. Competitors explore gradient boosting models, feature engineering on time-series behavioral data, and ranking metrics like the custom AMEX evaluation metric combining Gini and top-4% default rate recall. Highly relevant for practitioners working on credit risk, survival analysis, and tabular ML.

title: Bayesian Optimization in R
summary: A hands-on tutorial implementing Bayesian Optimization (BayesOpt) from scratch in R using Gaussian Processes as a surrogate model. Covers the core BayesOpt algorithm with pseudocode, and implements the three major acquisition functions: Probability of Improvement (PI), Expected Improvement (EI), and GP Lower Confidence Bound (LCB). Uses the GPfit package for Gaussian process fitting and demonstrates the approach on the Forrester (2008) benchmark function, with visualizations built in ggplot2 and gganimate.
topic: Bayesian Optimization with Gaussian Process surrogates

title: Acquisition Functions in Bayesian Optimization
summary: This resource provides a detailed explanation of acquisition functions used in Bayesian Optimization (BO), covering Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), Probability of Improvement (PI), and their mathematical formulations. It explains how acquisition functions work alongside a surrogate model (typically a Gaussian Process) to balance exploration vs. exploitation when optimizing black-box functions. The article includes visual demonstrations and tunable parameters, making it useful for practitioners implementing or tuning BO algorithms for hyperparameter optimization or experimental design.
